title: Are Passkeys Replacing Passwords in 2026? What You Need to Know
excerpt: Passkeys are the most significant shift in authentication in a decade — but passwords aren't gone yet. Here's the honest 2026 state of passkeys: where they work, where they don't, and what this means for your password manager.
content:
What Passkeys Actually Are

A passkey is a cryptographic key pair — one private key stored on your device, one public key stored on the website — that replaces your password entirely. Authentication happens locally on your device using biometrics (Face ID, fingerprint) or PIN, so nothing you know is ever transmitted or stored on a server. This is based on the WebAuthn/FIDO2 standard, developed by an industry consortium that includes Apple, Google, and Microsoft.

The security advantages are substantial: passkeys are immune to phishing (the private key is cryptographically bound to the website's domain, so a spoofed site gets nothing), immune to credential stuffing (there's no password to steal from a database breach), and resistant to brute-force attacks.

Where Passkeys Work in 2026

Passkey support has expanded dramatically. As of 2026, you can sign in with a passkey to: Apple ID, Google Account, Microsoft accounts, GitHub, PayPal, Amazon, Shopify, Coinbase, DocuSign, Best Buy, Kayak, and hundreds of others. Apple has made passkeys the default sign-in method on iOS 18 and macOS 15. Google now prompts users to create a passkey at every sign-in opportunity.

The cross-platform sync story has also improved: Apple Passwords syncs passkeys across all your Apple devices, Google Password Manager syncs across Android and Chrome, and third-party managers like Bitwarden and 1Password both support passkey storage, allowing passkeys to work across platforms.

What Still Requires Passwords in 2026

Despite rapid adoption, the majority of websites still require passwords. The long tail of smaller sites, enterprise SaaS tools, internal company systems, and legacy applications will take years to migrate. Most estimates put full passkey replacement of passwords at 2028–2030 for mainstream consumers, later for enterprise environments.

Even for services that support passkeys, you still need a password as a fallback — for account recovery, for logging in on a new device before passkey sync reaches it, or for services that only partially implement the standard.

Why You Still Need a Password Manager

Counterintuitively, the rise of passkeys increases the value of a good password manager, not decreases it. Here's why:

First, you still have hundreds of accounts with passwords, and that number won't drop to zero soon. Second, passkeys stored in 1Password or Bitwarden are cross-platform and cross-device in a way that platform-native passkeys (Apple, Google) aren't — a critical advantage if you use a mix of devices.

Third, recovery. If you rely solely on Apple Passwords for passkeys and lose access to your Apple ID, you lose your passkeys. A third-party manager gives you an independent recovery path.

Read our in-depth passkeys explainer for a technical walkthrough of how the FIDO2 protocol works.

How to Set Up Passkeys Today

On iPhone/Mac (Apple Passwords): Passkeys are automatic — when a supported site offers a passkey, iOS 18 prompts you to save it. You can view all saved passkeys in Settings → Passwords.

On Android/Chrome (Google Password Manager): Google will prompt you to create a passkey when you sign in to supported sites. Passkeys sync via your Google account across all Chrome/Android devices.

With 1Password or Bitwarden: Install the browser extension, sign in to a passkey-supporting site, and choose to save the passkey to your manager rather than to the browser. This keeps your passkeys in one place alongside your passwords.

Bottom Line

Passkeys are the future of authentication, and you should enable them everywhere they're available — they're strictly more secure than passwords. But they're not a replacement for a password manager yet. Use a manager like 1Password or Bitwarden to store both your passkeys and your remaining passwords in one secure, cross-platform vault.

Use our free password generator for any account that still requires a traditional password, and see our recommended tools for the full security toolkit.
